Wild Pastry. Heritage Ales. Off-Grid on the Canal.

A slow-fermented floating public house moored permanently at the historic Dundas Aqueduct.

The Brass & Thistle is not a traditional pub, nor is it a modern restaurant. It is an 80-foot, timber-fitted coal barge retrofitted with a hand-built clay cob oven, floating quietly on the Kennet & Avon Canal. We celebrate the ancient arts of wild fermentation, long-fermented pastry, and wood-fired cookery. Every pint we pump by hand is sourced from independent micro-breweries within walking distance of the towpath. Every pie we serve is built on a foundation of flour stone-ground just four miles away, then leavened with our native sourdough starter, active since 2021.

Seventy-Two Hours of Wild Patience

At the heart of our floating galley is a sourdough culture we call "Old Arthur," captured from the wild yeasts drifting through the Limpley Stoke valley. Unlike commercial pastry, which relies on fast-acting synthetic leavening, our crusts take three full days to develop. We use Maris Widgeon heritage wheat, a rare variety prized by traditional roof-thatchers for its tall stalks and by traditional bakers for its deep, nutty complexity.

The dough is laminated with organic pasture-fed lard, chilled by the canal's natural thermal mass below the waterline, and folded twenty-four times to produce an extraordinarily flaky, crisp crumb. When baked in the intense, radiant heat of our English oak-fired oven, the wild acids react with the animal fats to produce a deeply caramelized, dark golden crust that holds its crunch even when drenched in our bone marrow gravy. This is not fast food; it is an edible record of the valley's native microflora.

The Ironmaster's Feast & Heritage Brews

Our flagship offering is the Ironmaster's Stout & Ox-Cheek Trench-Pie, a culinary tribute to the heavy industrial history of the canal's stone aqueducts. We take grass-fed beef cheeks from local Somerset farms, dry-cure them in sea salt and wild thyme, and braise them slowly for fourteen hours in our wood oven. The braising liquid is composed of rich, unfined stout brewed by hand in nearby Bradford-on-Avon. As the heat mellows, the collagen in the ox cheeks melts into a dense, velvety gravy enriched with roasted marrow bones.

This rich stew is encased within our signature 72-hour wild-fermented pastry, shaped by hand in iron moulds. We pair this hefty pie with our cellar-temperature heritage ales, drawn from traditional wooden casks using manual beer engines. It is a meal designed to sustain canal-boat crews, long-distance walkers, and quiet seekers alike.

Sourced Along the Towpath

Our supply chain is defined by the speed of a walking horse. We do not rely on large haulage networks or corporate distribution hubs. Instead, our ingredients travel along the historic towpath or arrive by water. Our Maris Widgeon wheat is harvested at Somerset fields and milled using water power at the historic canal-side mills nearby. The wild garlic, wood sorrel, and watercress that garnish our plates are hand-foraged from the damp limestone banks of the Limpley Stoke valley. Our cheeses are crafted by artisan cheesemakers based in Wiltshire, aged in nearby natural caves, and brought to our mooring by cargo bicycle. Even our firewood is sustainably harvested ash and oak from managed canal-side woodlands. This hyper-local framework keeps our carbon footprint extraordinarily low while ensuring our menu changes in perfect harmony with the micro-seasons of the southern waterways.

Our Floating Public House

In 1972, this vessel was launched as a heavy-duty steel coal carrier, hauling raw fossil fuels across the industrial arteries of southern England. Today, she has been entirely repurposed as a sanctuary of slow living and culinary preservation. Transforming an eighty-foot rusting hull into an off-grid galley was an exercise in industrial reclamation and stubborn perseverance. Our greatest challenge was engineering a foundation capable of supporting a three-tonne hand-built clay cob oven on a floating steel platform without capsizing the narrowboat. We spent fourteen months insulating the holds, sourcing reclaimed English oak for the interior fittings, and installing high-yield solar arrays along the cabin roof to provide the minimal electricity our small pumps require. The result is a venue defined by off-grid grit, where the rhythmic lap of canal water replaces the hum of refrigeration, and where every meal is shaped entirely by the elements.

View Our Gallery

The Daily Board

Our chalkboard menu rotates continuously, dictated solely by what the local topography yields.

  • The Ironmaster's Stout & Ox-Cheek Trench-Pie £18.50
    The Shambles Micro-Brewery, Somerset

    Our flagship pastry. Pasture-raised Somerset beef cheeks slow-braised for fourteen hours in independent stout from Bradford-on-Avon.

  • The Poacher's Venison & Wild Juniper Pie £19.00
    Mendip Hills Estates

    Fallow deer culled from the managed estates, wrapped in our 72-hour wild sourdough crust with hand-foraged juniper berries, smoky bacon lardons, and a dense red currant reduction.

  • The Towpath Forager’s Mushroom & Chestnut Pie £17.50
    Gorge Farm Cheesemakers, Wiltshire

    A rich, vegetarian homage to the damp woodland banks. Wild chanterelles and oyster mushrooms from the Limpley Stoke valley, roasted Wiltshire chestnuts, and raw-milk Cheddar.

  • Copper Rivet Bitter £4.80
    Lockskeeper Brewing

    A traditional, uncarbonated heritage bitter drawn from traditional wooden casks.

  • Old Arthur's Dark Mild £5.00
    House Cask

    A historically accurate, low-ABV dark ale with deep molasses notes.

  • Aqueduct Pale £4.90
    Limpley Stoke Brewery

    Bright, zesty, and brewed with native hedgerow hops.

Check Today's Cask

Waterway Etiquette & Moorings

Q: How do we locate the barge at the Dundas Aqueduct?

A: We are moored precisely fifty yards south of the historic Dundas Aqueduct stonework, on the eastern towpath side of the Kennet & Avon Canal. Look for the twin brass lanterns and the wood smoke rising from the galley chimney.

Q: Can we moor our narrowboat directly alongside you?

A: Yes, we welcome visiting narrowboats. Please approach at a dead-slow pace to avoid disturbing our sourdough fermentation and use breast-lines to moor alongside our steel hull. The channel here is deep enough for historic drafts.

Q: What is the best walking route from Bath?

A: The most scenic approach is a flat, four-mile walk from Bath city centre along the towpath. It takes approximately ninety minutes at a moderate pace, passing under ancient bridges and through dense woodland.

Q: Is the floating pub accessible for those with limited mobility?

A: Due to the original 1970s steelwork and the narrow, steep companionway steps leading down into the hull, wheelchair access is regrettably impossible. However, we happily serve patrons on the flat, accessible towpath adjacent to our mooring.

Q: Are dogs permitted on board?

A: Well-behaved, muddy dogs are enthusiastically welcomed. We keep fresh canal-side water bowls and dehydrated ox-liver offcuts explicitly for our four-legged patrons.

Get Walking Directions

The Patron's Log

“The most remarkable pastry I have encountered in a decade of food writing. The crust possesses a deep, nutty complexity and an extraordinary flakiness that only a genuine 72-hour wild fermentation can achieve. The ox-cheek filling is astonishingly rich.” Thomas Wainright Food Columnist for The Somerset Herald
“Mooring alongside The Brass & Thistle in the freezing November fog was the highlight of our winter cruise. Stepping into that candlelit interior, smelling the wood smoke, and warming our hands over a flawless dark mild was pure magic.” Captain Sarah Jenkins Skipper of the Narrowboat Minstrel
“We hiked the six miles from Bradford-on-Avon in pouring rain. The hearty trench-pie and the cellar-temperature heritage ale waiting for us at the aqueduct completely revived our spirits. A brilliant, atmospheric experience.” David & Eleanor Thorne Long-distance Walkers
Leave a Log Entry

The Galley Hatch

The rhythm of our galley is entirely dictated by the biological reality of wild yeast. Consequently, we operate on a strictly unhurried schedule. We are open to the public from Wednesday through Sunday, from noon until the brass lanterns run out of oil (usually around ten at night). We are firmly closed on Mondays and Tuesdays to allow our sourdough starter, 'Old Arthur', his necessary fermentation rest and to replenish our firewood stocks.

We absolutely do not accept telephone reservations. Securing a table inside the candlelit hull or a barrel on the towpath is strictly on a walk-in basis, rewarding those who make the journey. For inquiries regarding exclusive private charters of the barge during our closed days, please direct correspondence to the master baker at charters@brassandthistle.co.uk.

Contact the Galley

© 2026 The Brass & Thistle.

Moored permanently at Dundas Aqueduct, Limpley Stoke, Bath, United Kingdom.
